mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Estonia on?
mapQuestions.answer: Europe, in the Baltic region. Estonia faces the Baltic Sea, with Latvia to the south, Russia to the east, and Finland just across the gulf to the north 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Estonia?
mapQuestions.answer: Tallinn, on the north coast. About 450,000 people. Famous for its almost perfectly-preserved medieval old town, a UNESCO World Heritage site.

mapQuestions.question: What language does Estonia speak?
mapQuestions.answer: Estonian, related closely to Finnish (and distantly to Hungarian). Estonian is famously hard for English speakers, with 14 different word endings ("cases"). Most younger Estonians also speak English and Russian.

mapQuestions.question: Why is Estonia so digital?
mapQuestions.answer: Estonia is one of the most digitally advanced countries on Earth. Estonians vote online, sign contracts online, get medical prescriptions online, and the country invented "e-Residency" in 2014 (anyone in the world can become a digital resident and run a company from Estonia).

mapQuestions.question: When did Estonia become independent?
mapQuestions.answer: Estonia first declared independence in 1918, was occupied by the Soviet Union from 1944, and regained independence in 1991. Joined the EU and NATO in 2004.

foods.name: Kartulisalat
foods.description: Estonian potato salad: boiled potatoes, pickled cucumber, smoked ham, hard-boiled egg and dill, dressed in light mayonnaise. The classic Estonian celebration side dish.

foods.name: Verivorst
foods.description: Black pudding made with barley, pork blood and onion, traditionally served at Christmas with lingonberry jam, sauerkraut and roast potatoes.

foods.name: Sprats on rye bread
foods.description: Smoked Baltic sprats (small silvery fish) on dark rye bread, with a slice of hard-boiled egg and a sprinkle of chives. The Estonian sandwich.

foods.name: Black bread
foods.description: Estonian "leib" is dark, dense rye bread that ferments for days. Estonians take leib so seriously there is a saying: "If you drop bread, kiss it before picking it up."

foods.name: Kama
foods.description: A unique Estonian flour made from roasted barley, rye, oat and pea, mixed with cold milk or yogurt for a sweet-savoury breakfast. Smells like cereal but ancient.

foods.name: Honey cake (medovik)
foods.description: Layers of thin honey sponge stacked with sour-cream filling. A Baltic-Russian classic, popular at every Tallinn café.

scavengerItems.label: The medieval walls and towers of Tallinn Old Town
The onion domes of Alexander Nevsky Cathedral on Toompea hill
Town Hall Square in Tallinn (the medieval centre, with the oldest pharmacy in Europe)
A pine forest path through Lahemaa National Park
Big "erratic" boulders left in the forest by the last Ice Age
A traditional smoke sauna ("suitsusaun") in southern Estonia
A Tallinn Song Festival ground (where 100,000+ Estonians sang together to win independence)
A digital-government kiosk (you can do almost any official task in Estonia by app)
A windmill on the island of Saaremaa
Wolf tracks in Lahemaa snow (Estonia has Europe's densest wolf population)
Cranes or white storks migrating across Matsalu wetlands in spring/autumn
The seaplane harbour museum with a real submarine inside

geoMatches.description: The capital. One of the best-preserved medieval old towns in Europe, with cobbled lanes, Gothic spires, defensive walls and towers, all surrounded by the modern city.
Estonia's second city, a quiet university town in the south with one of the oldest universities in northern Europe (1632).
A national park east of Tallinn: pine forests, bogs, boulder beaches, manor houses, and one of Europe's biggest brown bear populations.
Estonia's biggest island, with windmills, a medieval bishop's castle in Kuressaare, and a meteor crater (the Kaali crater, 7,500+ years old).
The southern Estonian "winter capital" with rolling hills, cross-country skiing, and one of Estonia's deepest holes (Otepää's biggest summer lake is also a winter ski-jump arena).
A coastal national park on Estonia's west coast, one of Europe's most important bird migration stopovers. Thousands of cranes, geese and swans pass through every spring and autumn.

senses.smell: Pine forest in summer, smoke from a "suitsusaun" smoke sauna, dark rye bread baking in a Tallinn bakery, sea-salt from the Baltic, fermenting cabbage in winter market jars…
senses.hear: "Tere!" greetings, the crunch of snow under boots in winter Tallinn, choirs practising the Estonian Song Festival songs, wind through Lahemaa pine forests, the seagulls of Pirita beach…
senses.taste: Smoky sprats on dark rye, sweet honey cake, sharp sauerkraut, warm Estonian black-bread porridge with butter, fresh wild forest berries crushed in summer…
senses.feel: The bite of -15°C Tallinn air in January, soft moss in Lahemaa pine forests, hot smoke-sauna heat, the polish of 800-year-old cobblestones, the cold of the Baltic in summer (still rarely above 18°C)…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why is Tallinn so well preserved?
stories.body: Tallinn's Old Town has barely changed since the 1400s. Stone city walls, 20 surviving defensive towers, Gothic spires, cobbled lanes, the Town Hall Square, all almost intact. Why? Tallinn was an important Hanseatic League trading port, made it rich, built strong city walls, then slowly stopped being important from the 1700s onward and was preserved by accident as the rest of Europe modernised around it. UNESCO put the Old Town on the World Heritage list in 1997. Today you can walk into a pharmacy operating at the same address since 1422 (the Raeapteek is one of the oldest continuously running pharmacies in Europe), drink coffee in a 700-year-old monk's wine cellar, and walk along walls that haven't moved in six centuries.

stories.question: What was the Singing Revolution?
stories.body: Estonia spent most of the 20th century occupied: first by Russia, then by Germany during World War II, then by the Soviet Union from 1944. Estonians never gave up their language, songs, or identity. Every five years the country holds a giant SONG FESTIVAL where 30,000+ singers in a single choir, in front of audiences of 100,000, sing traditional Estonian songs in unison. From 1988 to 1991, those gatherings turned into mass protests against Soviet rule. There was almost no violence. Estonians simply sang. In August 1991, around 2 million Estonians, Latvians and Lithuanians joined hands across all three countries in a 675 km human chain ("the Baltic Way"). A few weeks later, Soviet power collapsed. The whole movement is now called the "Singing Revolution".
stories.thinkingQuestion: A country regained its independence largely by singing. What other peaceful things might be more powerful than people realise?

stories.question: How digital is Estonia?
stories.body: Estonia is the most digitally advanced country in the world. Estonians vote online in national elections. Estonians sign legal documents online. Estonians get prescriptions online. 99% of all Estonian government services are available online. Estonia even invented "e-Residency" in 2014: anyone in the world (regardless of nationality) can become a digital resident of Estonia, get an Estonian digital ID card, and run an EU business from Estonia online, without ever living there. Over 100,000 people from 170+ countries have become e-Residents. The Estonian government claims it saves around 1,400 years of human time every year through digital services. Skype was invented in Estonia (Tallinn) in 2003.

stories.question: What is a meteor crater doing on Saaremaa?
stories.body: About 7,500 years ago, a meteor about the size of a school bus broke into pieces in the upper atmosphere over Estonia. Nine separate fragments hit the ground on what is now the island of Saaremaa, creating nine craters. The biggest one (Kaali Crater) is 110 metres across and 22 metres deep, now a circular lake. Iron Age Estonians remembered the event in their oral stories and used the crater as a sacred site for thousands of years. Kaali is one of the most accessible and best-preserved meteor craters in the world, and you can walk right up to its edge.

stories.question: What is a "soomaa" bog?
stories.body: Estonia is around 22% PEAT BOGS, one of the highest percentages of any country on Earth. These bogs are spongy, ancient, full of small lakes the colour of strong tea (stained brown by tannins from decaying plants). Many are 7,000+ years old. Walking on them requires special wooden plank paths or "bog shoes" (snowshoes for soft ground). In spring some Estonian families do "bog walks" at dawn, watching for cranes and moose, eating fresh wild cranberries straight from the moss. Estonia has more bogs than meadows, and the country is quietly proud of them.

convoQuestions.question: Best food in Estonia so far?
Old Town, forest, island, bog, which has felt the most "Estonia"?
Did you go in a sauna? How was it different from a regular one?
How does it feel walking around medieval streets that have not changed in 600 years?
Estonia gained independence by singing. What kind of peaceful protest could change something in your country?

wordSearch.hint: Estonian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Brown bear
animals.description: About 1,000 brown bears live wild in Estonian forests. Lahemaa National Park has guided summer bear-hide watches at dusk.

animals.name: Grey wolf
animals.description: Estonia has Europe's densest wolf population per square km. Around 200 wolves live in Estonian forests.

animals.name: Moose / elk
animals.description: The biggest deer species, common in Estonian forests. Sometimes crossing country roads at dawn.

animals.name: Common crane
animals.description: Big flocks gather in Matsalu National Park every autumn before migrating south. Stunning to watch.

animals.name: Wild boar
animals.description: Common across Estonian woods and farmland, especially in winter when they look for food in fields.

animals.name: European otter
animals.description: Returned to Estonian rivers and lakes. Best chance of spotting one is at dawn at a quiet riverside.

animals.name: Eurasian lynx
animals.description: Around 800 lynx live in Estonian forests, one of the densest populations in Europe. Hard to see.
